item.title: Complete guide to keyboard shortcuts on Mac
item.itunes:title: Complete guide to keyboard shortcuts on Mac
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Quick Links

  • Commonly used Mac shortcuts
  • Power shortcuts
  • Finder and system shortcuts
  • Document shortcuts

The latest Macs are powered by Apple silicon and macOS Sonoma, making them capable yet energy-efficient powerhouses. Their capability, however, doesn't explicitly revolve around the professional app suites they support. The computers themselves pack plenty of advanced features that enable users to make the most out of their time and resources. These include some key combinations that can execute useful tasks. Below, you will find a comprehensive list of the Mac's keyboard shortcuts.

Commonly used Mac shortcuts

Let's start with commonly used Mac shortcuts that users usually rely on when performing everyday tasks.

  • Command + X: Cut selected item and copy it to clipboard.
  • Command + C: Copy selected item to clipboard. This also works for files in Finder, not only text and photos.
  • Command + V: Paste clipboard items into current text field, document, window, or app.
  • Command + Z: Undo last command.
  • Shift + Command + Z: Redo last undone command.
  • Command + A: Select all items in a window, document, or text field.
  • Command + F: Find items in an open document or open a new Find window.
  • Command + G: Find the next occurrence of a found item.
  • Shift + Command + G: Find previous occurrence of a found item.
  • Command + H: Hide the windows of the front app.
  • Option + Command + H: Hide all apps except for the front window.
  • Command + M: Minimize the front window.
  • Option + Command + M: Minimize all windows of the front app.
  • Command + O: Open a selected item. If none are selected, bring up a prompt to choose a file to open.
  • Command + P: Print the opened document.
  • Command + S: Save the opened document.
  • Command + T: Open a new tab of the front app.
  • Command + W: Close the front window.
  • Option + Command + W: Close all windows of the front app.
  • Option + Command + Esc: Force quit an app. This is particularly useful when an app isn't responsive.
  • Command + Space bar: Bring up or close Spotlight Search.
  • Option + Command + Space bar: Perform a Spotlight search in a Finder window.
  • Control + Command + Space bar: Bring up the Character Viewer to insert emoji and other special characters.
  • Control + Command + F: Switch to a full screen window, if the app supports it.
  • Space bar: Use Quick Look on a selected item.
  • Command + Tab: Switch to the next most recently used (open) app.
  • Shift + Command + 5: Open the Screenshot app to take a screenshot or a screen recording.
  • Shift + Command + N: Create a new folder in Finder.
  • Command + comma: Open the settings of the front app.

Power shortcuts

We then have shortcuts that revolve around your Mac's power state. Though, please note that some of the shortcuts below don't apply to Macs with Touch ID Power buttons.

  • Power button:
    • Press to turn on your Mac if it's off or to wake it if it's asleep.
    • Press and hold for 1.5 seconds to put your Mac to sleep.
    • Press and hold for longer to force your Mac to turn off. This is particularly useful when your Mac is irresponsive.

  • Option + Command + Power button: Put your Mac to sleep.
  • Control + Shift + Power button: Put your display(s) to sleep.
  • Control + Power button: Bring up the Restart/Sleep/Shut Down prompt.
  • Control + Command + Power button: Force restart your Mac. Note that unsaved documents and changes will likely be lost. Use this only if your Mac is irresponsive.
  • Control + Option + Command + Power button: Quit all open apps, choose whether you want to keep unsaved changes, then Shut Down your Mac.
  • Control + Command + Q: Lock your Mac screen.
  • Shift + Command + Q: Log out of your macOS user account after confirming.
  • Option + Shift + Command: Log out of your macOS user account without confirming.

Finder and system shortcuts

You also have keyboard shortcuts that revolve around the Finder app and other system elements.

  • Command + D: Duplicate selected file(s).
  • Command + E: Eject selected disk or volume.
  • Command + F: Invoke Spotlight Search in a Finder window.
  • Command + I: Bring up the Get Info of a selected file.
  • Command + R:
    • Show the original file of a selected alias in Finder.
    • Reload the page in select apps, such as Safari.
    • Check for software updates in Software Update settings.

  • Shift + Command + C: Open the Computer folder.
  • Shift + Command + D: Open the Desktop folder.
  • Shift + Command + F: Open the Recents folder.
  • Shift + Command + G: Open the Go to Folder dialogue.
  • Shift + Command + H: Open the Home folder of the current macOS user.
  • Shift + Command + I: Open the iCloud Drive folder.
  • Shift + Command + K: Open the Network folder.
  • Option + Command + L: Open the Downloads folder.
  • Shift + Command + N: Create a new folder.
  • Shift + Command + O: Open the Documents folder.
  • Shift + Command + P: Show/hide the Preview pane in Finder windows.
  • Shift + Command + R: Open the AirDrop window.
  • Shift + Command + T: Show/hide the tab bar in Finder windows.
  • Control + Shift + Command + T: Add selected item in Finder to the Dock
  • Shift + Command + U: Open the Utilities folder.
  • Option + Command + D: Show/hide the Dock.
  • Control + Command + T: Add selected item to the sidebar.
  • Option + Command + P: Hide/show the path bar in a Finder window.
  • Option + Command + S: Hide/show the sidebar in a Finder window.
  • Command + Slash: Hide/show the status bar in a Finder window.
  • Command + J: Show the View Options.
  • Command + K: Open the Connect to Server window.
  • Control + Command + A: Set an alias to a selected item.
  • Command + N: Open a new Finder window.
  • Option + Command + N: Create a new Smart Folder in Finder.
  • Command + T: Show/hide the tab bar when only one Finder tab is open in the current window.
  • Option + Command + T: Show/hide the toolbar when only one Finder tab is open in the current window.
  • Option + Command + V: Move Clipboard files from their initial source to the current location.
  • Command + Y: Use Quick Look on a selected item.
  • Option + Command + Y: Use a Quick Look slideshow on multiple selected item.
  • Command + 1: Switch to Icon View in a Finder window.
  • Command + 2: Switch to List View in a Finder window.
  • Command + 3: Switch to Column View in a Finder window.
  • Command + 4: Switch to Gallery View in a Finder window.
  • Command + Left Bracket: Go to previous folder.
  • Command + Right Bracket: Go to next folder.
  • Command + Up Arrow: Open the folder containing the current one.
  • Command + Control + Up Arrow: Open the folder containing the current one in a new Finder window.
  • Command + Down Arrow: Open the selected item.
  • Right Arrow: When in List View, open the selected folder.
  • Left Arrow: When in List View, close the selected folder.
  • Command + Delete: Trash selected item(s).
  • Shift + Command + Delete: Empty the Trash, if it has any items, after you confirm.
  • Option + Shift + Command + Delete: Empty the Trash, if it has any items, without your confirmation.
  • Command + Brightness Down: Toggle video mirroring if your Mac is connected to more than one device.
  • Option + Brightness Up/Down: Open Displays settings.
  • Control + Brightness Up: Turn up the brightness on a supported external display.
  • Control + Brightness Down: Turn down the brightness on a supported external display.
  • Option + Shift + Brightness Up: Turn up the brightness in smaller steps.
  • Option + Shift + Brightness Down: Turn down the brightness in smaller steps.
  • Control + Option + Shift + Brightness Up: Turn up the brightness in smaller steps on a supported external display.
  • Control + Option + Shift + Brightness Down: Turn down the brightness in smaller steps on a supported external display.
  • Option + Mission Control: Open Mission Control settings.
  • Command + Mission Control: Show the desktop.
  • Control + Down Arrow: Show all open windows of the front app.
  • Option + Volume Up/Down: Open Sound settings.
  • Option + Shift + Volume Up: Turn up the volume in smaller steps.
  • Option + Shift + Volume Down: Turn down the volume in smaller steps.
  • Option + Keyboard Brightness Up/Down: Open Keyboard settings.
  • Option + Shift + Keyboard Brightness Up: Turn up the keyboard brightness in smaller steps.
  • Option + Shift + Keyboard Brightness Down: Turn down the keyboard brightness in smaller steps.
  • Option + Double Click: Open item in a new window and close original one.
  • Command + Double Click: Open a folder in a separate tab/window.
  • Command + Drag to another volume: Move dragged item to new volume rather than copying it.
  • Option + Drag: Copy dragged item.
  • Option + Command + Drag: Set an alias for the dragged item.
  • Command + Click window title: When in List View, open all folders within a selected folder.

Document shortcuts

Lastly, there are dedicated shortcuts for when you're editing or interacting with a certain document on your Mac.

  • Command + B: Toggle boldfacing, or boldface selected text.
  • Command + I: Toggle italics, or italicize selected text.
  • Command + K: Add a URL.
  • Command + U: Toggle underlining, or underline selected text.
  • Command + T: Show/hide the Fonts window.
  • Command + D: Select the Desktop folder when presented with an Open/Save dialogue.
  • Control + Command + D: Show/hide the definition of a selected word.
  • Shift + Command + Colon: Show the Spelling and Grammar window.
  • Command + Semicolon: Find misspelled words in an open document.
  • Option + Delete: Delete the word to the left of the cursor.
  • Control + H: Delete the character to the left of the cursor. Alternatively, you could just click Delete.
  • Control + D: Delete the character to the right of the cursor.
  • Control + K: Delete all text between cursor and the end of a line/paragraph.
  • Fn + Up Arrow: Scroll one page up (Page Up).
  • Fn + Down Arrow: Scroll one page down (Page Down).
  • Fn + Left Arrow: Scroll to the beginning of a document (Home).
  • Fn + Right Arrow: Scroll to the end of a document (End).
  • Command + Up Arrow: Move the cursor to the beginning of a document.
  • Command + Down Arrow: Move the cursor to the end of a document.
  • Command + Left Arrow: Move the cursor to the beginning of the current line.
  • Command + Right Arrow: Move the cursor to the end of the current line.
  • Option + Left Arrow: Move the cursor to the beginning of the previous word.
  • Option + Right Arrow: Move the cursor to the beginning of the next word.
  • Shift + Command + Up Arrow: Select the text between the cursor and the beginning of a document.
  • Shift + Command + Down Arrow: Select the text between the cursor and the end of a document.
  • Shift + Command + Left Arrow: Select the text between the cursor and the beginning of the current line.
  • Shift + Command + Right Arrow: Select the text between the cursor and the end of the current line.
  • Shift +Up Arrow: Extend text selection to the nearest character on the same horizontal level on the line above.
  • Shift + Down Arrow: Extend text selection to the nearest character on the same horizontal level on the line below.
  • Shift + Left Arrow: Extend text selection one character to the left.
  • Shift + Right Arrow: Extend text selection one character to the right.
  • Option + Shift + Up Arrow: Extend text selection to the beginning of current paragraph. Pressing it again will extend it to the beginning of the following paragraph.
  • Option + Shift + Down Arrow: Extend text selection to the end of current paragraph. Pressing it again will extend it to the end of the following paragraph.
  • Option + Shift + Left Arrow: Extend text selection to the beginning of current word. Pressing it again will extend it to the beginning of the following word.
  • Option + Shift + Right Arrow: Extend text selection to the end of current word. Pressing it again will extend it to the end of the following word.
  • Control + A: Move the cursor to the beginning of the line or paragraph.
  • Control + E: Move the cursor to the end of the line or paragraph.
  • Control + F: Move the cursor one character forward.
  • Control + B: Move the cursor one character backward.
  • Control + L: Center the cursor or selected text in the visible area.
  • Control + P: Move the cursor one line up.
  • Control + N: Move the cursor one line down.
  • Control + O: Insert a new line after the cursor.
  • Control + T: Swap the characters behind and in front of the cursor.
  • Command + Left Curly Bracket: Align left.
  • Command + Right Curly Bracket: Align right.
  • Shift + Command + Vertical Bar: Align center.
  • Option + Command + F: Go to search field.
  • Option + Command + T: Show/hide the toolbar.
  • Option + Command + C: Copy selected text to Clipboard including the formatting.
  • Option + Command + V: Paste text from Clipboard including the formatting.
  • Shift + Option + Command + V: Paste text from Clipboard while matching the surrounding text's formatting.
  • Option + Command + I: Show/hide the inspector window.
  • Shift + Command + P: Bring up a window to select document settings.
  • Shift + Command + S: Bring up the Save As prompt or duplicate the current document.
  • Shift + Command + Minus: Decrease the size of a selected item.
  • Shift + Command + Plus: Increase the size of a selected item.
  • Command + Equal: Increase the size of a selected item.
  • Shift + Command + Question Mark: Bring up the Help menu.

That's everything you need to know about keyboard shortcuts on a Mac! As you can see, there are dozens them, with each serving a unique purpose. While we don't expect you to memorize them all by heart, your muscle memory may automatically register the ones you actively use. In the meantime, you can bookmark this webpage to easily access it whenever you're seeking the key combination of a certain shortcut on your Mac.

Quick Links

The latest Macs are powered by Apple silicon and macOS Sonoma, making them capable yet energy-efficient powerhouses. Their capability, however, doesn't explicitly revolve around the professional app suites they support. The computers themselves pack plenty of advanced features that enable users to make the most out of their time and resources. These include some key combinations that can execute useful tasks. Below, you will find a comprehensive list of the Mac's keyboard shortcuts.

Commonly used Mac shortcuts

Let's start with commonly used Mac shortcuts that users usually rely on when performing everyday tasks.

  • Command + X: Cut selected item and copy it to clipboard.

We then have shortcuts that revolve around your Mac's power state. Though, please note that some of the shortcuts below don't apply to Macs with Touch ID Power buttons.

  • Power button:
    • Press to turn on your Mac if it's off or to wake it if it's asleep.
    • Press and hold for 1.5 seconds to put your Mac to sleep.
    • Press and hold for longer to force your Mac to turn off. This is particularly useful when your Mac is irresponsive.

You also have keyboard shortcuts that revolve around the Finder app and other system elements.

  • Command + F: Invoke Spotlight Search in a Finder window.

  • Command + R:

  • Shift + Command + T: Show/hide the tab bar in Finder windows.
  • Control + Shift + Command + T: Add selected item in Finder to the Dock
  • Shift + Command + U: Open the Utilities folder.

  • Option + Command + S: Hide/show the sidebar in a Finder window.
  • Command + Slash: Hide/show the status bar in a Finder window.
  • Command + J: Show the View Options.
  • Command + K: Open the Connect to Server window.

  • Option + Volume Up/Down: Open Sound settings.

Document shortcuts

  • Command + K: Add a URL.
  • Command + U: Toggle underlining, or underline selected text.

  • Command + D: Select the Desktop folder when presented with an Open/Save dialogue.

  • Control + K: Delete all text between cursor and the end of a line/paragraph.

  • Finder and system shortcuts

  • Command + P: Print the opened document.
  • Command + S: Save the opened document.
  • Command + T: Open a new tab of the front app.
  • Command + W: Close the front window.
  • Option + Command + W: Close all windows of the front app.
  • Option + Command + Esc: Force quit an app. This is particularly useful when an app isn't responsive.
  • Command + Space bar: Bring up or close Spotlight Search.
  • Option + Command + Space bar: Perform a Spotlight search in a Finder window.
  • Control + Command + Space bar: Bring up the Character Viewer to insert emoji and other special characters.
  • Control + Command + F: Switch to a full screen window, if the app supports it.
  • Space bar: Use Quick Look on a selected item.
  • Command + Tab: Switch to the next most recently used (open) app.
  • Shift + Command + 5: Open the Screenshot app to take a screenshot or a screen recording.
  • Shift + Command + N: Create a new folder in Finder.
  • Command + comma: Open the settings of the front app.

Power shortcuts

We then have shortcuts that revolve around your Mac's power state. Though, please note that some of the shortcuts below don't apply to Macs with Touch ID Power buttons.

  • Power button:
    • Press to turn on your Mac if it's off or to wake it if it's asleep.
    • Press and hold for 1.5 seconds to put your Mac to sleep.
    • Press and hold for longer to force your Mac to turn off. This is particularly useful when your Mac is irresponsive.

  • Option + Command + Power button: Put your Mac to sleep.
  • Control + Shift + Power button: Put your display(s) to sleep.
  • Control + Power button: Bring up the Restart/Sleep/Shut Down prompt.
  • Control + Command + Power button: Force restart your Mac. Note that unsaved documents and changes will likely be lost. Use this only if your Mac is irresponsive.
  • Control + Option + Command + Power button: Quit all open apps, choose whether you want to keep unsaved changes, then Shut Down your Mac.
  • Control + Command + Q: Lock your Mac screen.
  • Shift + Command + Q: Log out of your macOS user account after confirming.
  • Option + Shift + Command: Log out of your macOS user account without confirming.

Finder and system shortcuts

You also have keyboard shortcuts that revolve around the Finder app and other system elements.

  • Command + D: Duplicate selected file(s).
  • Command + E: Eject selected disk or volume.
  • Command + F: Invoke Spotlight Search in a Finder window.
  • Command + I: Bring up the Get Info of a selected file.
  • Command + R:
    • Show the original file of a selected alias in Finder.
    • Reload the page in select apps, such as Safari.
    • Check for software updates in Software Update settings.

  • Shift + Command + C: Open the Computer folder.
  • Shift + Command + D: Open the Desktop folder.
  • Shift + Command + F: Open the Recents folder.
  • Shift + Command + G: Open the Go to Folder dialogue.
  • Shift + Command + H: Open the Home folder of the current macOS user.
  • Shift + Command + I: Open the iCloud Drive folder.
  • Shift + Command + K: Open the Network folder.
  • Option + Command + L: Open the Downloads folder.
  • Shift + Command + N: Create a new folder.
  • Shift + Command + O: Open the Documents folder.
  • Shift + Command + P: Show/hide the Preview pane in Finder windows.
  • Shift + Command + R: Open the AirDrop window.
  • Shift + Command + T: Show/hide the tab bar in Finder windows.
  • Control + Shift + Command + T: Add selected item in Finder to the Dock
  • Shift + Command + U: Open the Utilities folder.
  • Option + Command + D: Show/hide the Dock.
  • Control + Command + T: Add selected item to the sidebar.
  • Option + Command + P: Hide/show the path bar in a Finder window.
  • Option + Command + S: Hide/show the sidebar in a Finder window.
  • Command + Slash: Hide/show the status bar in a Finder window.
  • Command + J: Show the View Options.
  • Command + K: Open the Connect to Server window.
  • Control + Command + A: Set an alias to a selected item.
  • Command + N: Open a new Finder window.
  • Option + Command + N: Create a new Smart Folder in Finder.
  • Command + T: Show/hide the tab bar when only one Finder tab is open in the current window.
  • Option + Command + T: Show/hide the toolbar when only one Finder tab is open in the current window.
  • Option + Command + V: Move Clipboard files from their initial source to the current location.
  • Command + Y: Use Quick Look on a selected item.
  • Option + Command + Y: Use a Quick Look slideshow on multiple selected item.
  • Command + 1: Switch to Icon View in a Finder window.
  • Command + 2: Switch to List View in a Finder window.
  • Command + 3: Switch to Column View in a Finder window.
  • Command + 4: Switch to Gallery View in a Finder window.
  • Command + Left Bracket: Go to previous folder.
  • Command + Right Bracket: Go to next folder.
  • Command + Up Arrow: Open the folder containing the current one.
  • Command + Control + Up Arrow: Open the folder containing the current one in a new Finder window.
  • Command + Down Arrow: Open the selected item.
  • Right Arrow: When in List View, open the selected folder.
  • Left Arrow: When in List View, close the selected folder.
  • Command + Delete: Trash selected item(s).
  • Shift + Command + Delete: Empty the Trash, if it has any items, after you confirm.
  • Option + Shift + Command + Delete: Empty the Trash, if it has any items, without your confirmation.
  • Command + Brightness Down: Toggle video mirroring if your Mac is connected to more than one device.
  • Option + Brightness Up/Down: Open Displays settings.
  • Control + Brightness Up: Turn up the brightness on a supported external display.
  • Control + Brightness Down: Turn down the brightness on a supported external display.
  • Option + Shift + Brightness Up: Turn up the brightness in smaller steps.
  • Option + Shift + Brightness Down: Turn down the brightness in smaller steps.
  • Control + Option + Shift + Brightness Up: Turn up the brightness in smaller steps on a supported external display.
  • Control + Option + Shift + Brightness Down: Turn down the brightness in smaller steps on a supported external display.
  • Option + Mission Control: Open Mission Control settings.
  • Command + Mission Control: Show the desktop.
  • Control + Down Arrow: Show all open windows of the front app.
  • Option + Volume Up/Down: Open Sound settings.
  • Option + Shift + Volume Up: Turn up the volume in smaller steps.
  • Option + Shift + Volume Down: Turn down the volume in smaller steps.
  • Option + Keyboard Brightness Up/Down: Open Keyboard settings.
  • Option + Shift + Keyboard Brightness Up: Turn up the keyboard brightness in smaller steps.
  • Option + Shift + Keyboard Brightness Down: Turn down the keyboard brightness in smaller steps.
  • Option + Double Click: Open item in a new window and close original one.
  • Command + Double Click: Open a folder in a separate tab/window.
  • Command + Drag to another volume: Move dragged item to new volume rather than copying it.
  • Option + Drag: Copy dragged item.
  • Option + Command + Drag: Set an alias for the dragged item.
  • Command + Click window title: When in List View, open all folders within a selected folder.

Document shortcuts

Lastly, there are dedicated shortcuts for when you're editing or interacting with a certain document on your Mac.

  • Command + B: Toggle boldfacing, or boldface selected text.
  • Command + I: Toggle italics, or italicize selected text.
  • Command + K: Add a URL.
  • Command + U: Toggle underlining, or underline selected text.
  • Command + T: Show/hide the Fonts window.
  • Command + D: Select the Desktop folder when presented with an Open/Save dialogue.
  • Control + Command + D: Show/hide the definition of a selected word.
  • Shift + Command + Colon: Show the Spelling and Grammar window.
  • Command + Semicolon: Find misspelled words in an open document.
  • Option + Delete: Delete the word to the left of the cursor.
  • Control + H: Delete the character to the left of the cursor. Alternatively, you could just click Delete.
  • Control + D: Delete the character to the right of the cursor.
  • Control + K: Delete all text between cursor and the end of a line/paragraph.
  • Fn + Up Arrow: Scroll one page up (Page Up).
  • Fn + Down Arrow: Scroll one page down (Page Down).
  • Fn + Left Arrow: Scroll to the beginning of a document (Home).
  • Fn + Right Arrow: Scroll to the end of a document (End).
  • Command + Up Arrow: Move the cursor to the beginning of a document.
  • Command + Down Arrow: Move the cursor to the end of a document.
  • Command + Left Arrow: Move the cursor to the beginning of the current line.
  • Command + Right Arrow: Move the cursor to the end of the current line.
  • Option + Left Arrow: Move the cursor to the beginning of the previous word.
  • Option + Right Arrow: Move the cursor to the beginning of the next word.
  • Shift + Command + Up Arrow: Select the text between the cursor and the beginning of a document.
  • Shift + Command + Down Arrow: Select the text between the cursor and the end of a document.
  • Shift + Command + Left Arrow: Select the text between the cursor and the beginning of the current line.
  • Shift + Command + Right Arrow: Select the text between the cursor and the end of the current line.
  • Shift +Up Arrow: Extend text selection to the nearest character on the same horizontal level on the line above.
  • Shift + Down Arrow: Extend text selection to the nearest character on the same horizontal level on the line below.
  • Shift + Left Arrow: Extend text selection one character to the left.
  • Shift + Right Arrow: Extend text selection one character to the right.
  • Option + Shift + Up Arrow: Extend text selection to the beginning of current paragraph. Pressing it again will extend it to the beginning of the following paragraph.
  • Option + Shift + Down Arrow: Extend text selection to the end of current paragraph. Pressing it again will extend it to the end of the following paragraph.
  • Option + Shift + Left Arrow: Extend text selection to the beginning of current word. Pressing it again will extend it to the beginning of the following word.
  • Option + Shift + Right Arrow: Extend text selection to the end of current word. Pressing it again will extend it to the end of the following word.
  • Control + A: Move the cursor to the beginning of the line or paragraph.
  • Control + E: Move the cursor to the end of the line or paragraph.
  • Control + F: Move the cursor one character forward.
  • Control + B: Move the cursor one character backward.
  • Control + L: Center the cursor or selected text in the visible area.
  • Control + P: Move the cursor one line up.
  • Control + N: Move the cursor one line down.
  • Control + O: Insert a new line after the cursor.
  • Control + T: Swap the characters behind and in front of the cursor.
  • Command + Left Curly Bracket: Align left.
  • Command + Right Curly Bracket: Align right.
  • Shift + Command + Vertical Bar: Align center.
  • Option + Command + F: Go to search field.
  • Option + Command + T: Show/hide the toolbar.
  • Option + Command + C: Copy selected text to Clipboard including the formatting.
  • Option + Command + V: Paste text from Clipboard including the formatting.
  • Shift + Option + Command + V: Paste text from Clipboard while matching the surrounding text's formatting.
  • Option + Command + I: Show/hide the inspector window.
  • Shift + Command + P: Bring up a window to select document settings.
  • Shift + Command + S: Bring up the Save As prompt or duplicate the current document.
  • Shift + Command + Minus: Decrease the size of a selected item.
  • Shift + Command + Plus: Increase the size of a selected item.
  • Command + Equal: Increase the size of a selected item.
  • Shift + Command + Question Mark: Bring up the Help menu.

That's everything you need to know about keyboard shortcuts on a Mac! As you can see, there are dozens them, with each serving a unique purpose. While we don't expect you to memorize them all by heart, your muscle memory may automatically register the ones you actively use. In the meantime, you can bookmark this webpage to easily access it whenever you're seeking the key combination of a certain shortcut on your Mac.

item.title: Best cases for Samsung Galaxy S23 FE in 2023
item.itunes:title: Best cases for Samsung Galaxy S23 FE in 2023
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Samsung is back in the value flagship game with the Galaxy S23 FE, and this is a great smartphone for those who want a high-end experience on a budget. It comes with a Snapdragon 8 Gen 1 processor and solid specs overall, but it starts at a significantly lower price than the standard Samsung Galaxy S23. However, just because it's affordable, it doesn't mean you shouldn't protect the Samsung Galaxy S23 FE with a case.

It's always worth investing in some coverage for your smartphone. Whether you need something relatively simple for drops and bumps, you want to go all-out, or you need something more unique with extra features, there's no shortage of options on the market. item.title: Early Black Friday deal knocks $200 off our favorite curved OLED gaming monitor, falling to its lowest price ever
item.itunes:title: Early Black Friday deal knocks $200 off our favorite curved OLED gaming monitor, falling to its lowest price ever
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Source: Dell
Alienware AW3423DWF
$800 $1000 Save $200

Dell's Alienware AW3423DWF is an exceptional 34-inch ultrawide gaming monitor with 3440x1440 resolution, 165Hz refresh rate, and 0.1ms response time. Its QD-OLED tech brings extra brightness and color, and it can be used for creative work when you aren't gaming.

$800 at Dell $800 at Best Buy

This Alienware AW3423DWF is one of the best curved monitors out right now. It offers vibrant colors thanks to its OLED panel and also has an impressive refresh rate that comes in at 165Hz with a response time of 0.1ms. While it's aimed towards gamers, it can easily handle anything you can throw at it, whether that's punching up emails, browsing the web for hours at a time, editing photos, and more. While this model typically comes in at $1000, we're seeing a fantastic discount right now that drops the price by $200 for a limited time.

What's great about Alienware's 34-inch AW3423DWF monitor?

This Alienware AW3423DWF monitor excels at everything, coming in with a sleek design and plenty of screen real estate that spans 34-inches. You get vibrant and beautiful colors thanks to the monitor's OLED panel that can reach up to 1000 nits at peak brightness and has TRUEBlack 400 certification. In addition, the curved panel offers an immersive experience that's also easy on the eyes, with impressive performance thanks to the 165Hz refresh rate, 0.01ms response time, and support for AMD's FreeSync Premium Pro.

With a monitor like this, you get ample connectivity options, with it offering DisplayPort, HDMI, and USB-C. And those looking to add a little flair to their desk setup will be happy to know that this monitor offers customizable RGB lighting on the rear which can really add some nice lighting accents to a room. While being a top-tier monitor is great, reliability is important, and luckily, Alienware offers impressive warranty with this model that comes in at three years and also covers panel burn-in.

Why buy Alienware's 34-inch AW3423DWF monitor?

If you're looking for a great OLED monitor, this really is the one. The Alienware AW3423DWF monitor is a top-performer and one of our favorite curved monitors of 2023. On top of the deep $200 discount that brings it down to its lowest price yet, those shopping at Best Buy can also take advantage of 24 month financing using the retailer's credit card and the company's return policy is now extended to January 13 for the holiday shopping season.

item.title: Microsoft Authenticator now suppresses shady login attempts
item.itunes:title: Microsoft Authenticator now suppresses shady login attempts
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Microsoft Authenticator suppresses shady login attempts by detecting anomalous signals and asking users to manually confirm the login.
  • This approach reduces unwanted notifications and increases security, preventing MFA fatigue attacks.
  • The implementation has been silently implemented on the backend, benefiting many users without their knowledge.

Microsoft Authenticator is a fairly versatile and convenient app - albeit with some issues - offering multi-factor authentication (MFA) capabilities for various services. The Redmond tech firm also heavily promotes it as a way to go completely passwordless on your Microsoft account. However, the cybersecurity landscape evolves dynamically, and now, Microsoft has detailed how it is suppressing shady login attempts via Authenticator itself.

A blog post penned by Microsoft's VP Director of Identity Security Alex Weinert describes how the company has been successfully suppressing anomalous login attempts that serve pop-up notifications via Authenticator. The rollout for this process was completed in September, with over six million unwanted login attempts - mostly hacker-initiated - being prevented since then.

Basically, under normal circumstances, if you attempt to log in to an account or a service, you'll be asked to approve the sign-in attempt via a pop-up Authenticator notification. However, if Microsoft detects some anomalous signals in the login attempt, such as origination from an unfamiliar location, the firm will intentionally suppress the attempt and you won't receive a notification. Instead, you'll be asked to manually open the app and confirm the login attempt. It is important to note that only the pop-up notification is suppressed, the login attempt isn't deleted.

Microsoft has emphasized that the implementation of this approach has led to fewer unwanted notifications being sent to end-customers, which not only ensures smoother workflows but also increases security. This process also reduces the chances of MFA fatigue attacks, in which a malicious actor gains access to your credentials and then continuously sends MFA prompts to you until you accidentally approve an attempt and give access to your account. It's also interesting to see that this is a change that has been silently implemented on the backend without much fanfare, which means that many customers have likely been reaping the benefits of this implementation without even realizing it.

Microsoft has emphasized that the implementation of this approach has led to fewer unwanted notifications being sent to end-customers, which not only ensures smoother workflows but also increases security. This process also reduces the chances of MFA fatigue attacks, in which a malicious actor gains access to your credentials and then continuously sends MFA prompts to you until you accidentally approve an attempt and give access to your account. It's also interesting to see that this is a change that has been silently implemented on the backend without much fanfare, which means that many customers have likely been reaping the benefits of this implementation without even realizing it.

item.title: Best iPad games in 2023
item.itunes:title: Best iPad games in 2023
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Quick Links

  • Role-playing
  • Adventure
  • Simulation
  • Puzzle
  • Battle royale
  • Card
  • Casual
  • Strategy

The latest iPads are undoubtedly the best tablets available for most users. Not only do they offer a feature-rich operating system, but they also support a large library of well-optimized apps. These applications include a wide variety of solid games that run excellently on the Apple tablet. That's particularly true on M-powered iPads, which take mobile gaming to the next level. To help you make the most out of your device, we've listed some of the best iPad games currently available.

Role-playing

Stardew Valley

For plenty of reasons, Stardew Valley is my favorite iPad game, but it's also one that fans of the pixelated RPG format and life simulators will appreciate. In this game, you have to restore, renovate, take care of, and populate a rural farm after moving away from the city and your dreadful 9-to-5 job. You can plant, raise cattle, interact with fellow townies, fish, mine, date, cast spells, and much, much more.

What further makes the game so special though is that it was created mostly by one person — Eric Barone, known as Concerned Ape. He not only designed the gameplay but also did the sound effects, original music, and graphics, creating a sense of harmony between its elements — complete coherence that you wouldn't usually find elsewhere. No matter how much you explore in this game, there's a high chance you're not even close to going through it all. But you can still play at your own pace. Even if you miss a certain goal or event, you can get it done at a later point. The game won't send you push notifications either, and time stops when you're not actually playing — so there's no pressure to play when you don't want to.

Stardew Valley costs $4.99 and includes no ads or in-app purchases. It additionally offers a local backup and restore option, so you can play the iPad version on the go, then manually copy your progress to your Mac or PC at home.

  • Download Stardew Valley

Genshin Impact

If you're a fan of anime-style RPGs, Genshin Impact is for you. It's a title with very rich gameplay and a solid storyline, where you have to explore a wide, beautifully crafted world, harness the seven elements (Anemo, Electro, Hydro, Pyro, Cryo, Geo, and Dendro), and fight deities. The storyline is meticulously crafted if you pay attention to it, the characters have depth to them, and while there is a certain element of button-mashing, there can be equal elements of team building and strategy, too. The open-world exploration is also one of the highlights of this game, and you can spend hours trying to solve puzzles to unlock further parts of the storyline.

Genshin Impact is available as a free download and supports multiplayer gameplay. However, the game has optional in-app purchases that can get quite costly if you're not careful. It's still enjoyable without paying a cent, nonetheless.

  • Download Genshin Impact

Adventure

Don't Starve: Pocket Edition

In Don't Starve the aim is... not starving. It's a survival game where you have to explore, collect items and food, cook, build equipment, fight monsters, and keep a bonfire burning through the night (trust me on this one). There are three main meters that you have to keep an eye on — hunger, sanity, and health — and if any of them hit zero, you're in for a bad time, especially when it comes to health. If monsters (even the cute ones) attack you, you will start losing health points, until you ultimately die and start over.

This game has basic touch controls and costs $4.99 in the App Store. It includes no in-app purchases, ads, or pay-to-win obstacles. If you're subscribed to Apple Arcade, you can avoid paying the $4.99 by playing Don't Starve: Pocket Edition+ for free. If you're a fan of the graphics, sound effects, and gameplay, you can pay another $4.99 for the Shipwrecked edition, which includes new worlds, resources, and creatures that want to kill you.

  • Download Don't Starve: Pocket Edition
  • Download Don't Starve: Pocket Edition+
  • Download Don't Starve: Shipwrecked

The Binding of Isaac: Rebirth

The Binding of Isaac is one of the sickest games out there, but in the best way. Here, you have to cry your way through endless basements until you defeat your mom — and ultimately the devil, too. If you're unfamiliar with this game, I'm assuming my brief description makes no sense to you, but that's sort of the point.

Isaac is a child who is running away from his murderous mom. Through underground basements, you have to find your way and kill monsters and bosses by shooting your tears at them. There are plenty of power-ups, secrets, and hidden gems that really make this game stand out. Just like Don't Starve, when you die, you have to start over in a uniquely generated setting.

The Binding of Isaac: Rebirth is available for $14.99 and includes no ads. There are some optional DLCs that you can buy separately through in-app purchases, but the base game still offers a ton of content on its own. This title offers virtual joystick controls for movement and shooting, which make it easily playable on the iPad's touchscreen.

  • Download The Binding of Isaac: Rebirth

Bastion

Why Bastion is on the list of best iPad games should be self-explanatory. It's one of the greatest computer games that's been ported to mobile. It's an action role-playing game (RPG) where you have to fight your way through different worlds and uncover how the world has been shattered. There are over 10 upgradeable weapons and six Bastion structures included, with the gameplay becoming more engaging as you progress further. Its graphics are detailed yet they're not super realistic, so they offer some artistic depth without changing the overall atmosphere of the game's setting. Once you finish the main story, you also get to play an unlockable mode to make more out of the game.

Bastion is available as a free download. However, you will need to unlock the full game as an in-app purchase after you go through its beginning. Think of it as a free demo, with the full game costing $4.99 (as a one-time purchase). The game has no ads or other IAPs — as you'd expect from a premium release.

  • Download Bastion

Plants vs. Zombies 2

The starved zombies are approaching, and they desire a taste of your human brains. You're in your house and zombies are coming through the front/backyard. You have to place all sorts of superplants to fight off the zombies and protect your delicate brains. Each plant has a certain cost, superpower, and health bar. It's all about placing them strategically to make sure no zombie gruesomely devours your head.

Plants vs. Zombies 2 makes sense on an iPad because its gameplay depends on tapping, dragging, and swiping. So the Apple tablet's touchscreen is more than enough to make the most out of it. In fact, I find it easier to play this game on an iPad rather than a Mac or PC. It's available as a free download but includes optional in-app purchases.

  • Download Plants vs. Zombies 2

Divinity: Original Sin 2

Divinity: Original Sin 2 is an excellent iPad game for those seeking an action-packed RPG. You get to create and customize your own hero and have the surrounding world react accordingly. The exciting aspect to this game is the ability to play and explore freely. You can wander wherever you want, talk to anyone, kill NPCs, and more. You also get to utilize different control methods, including touch, keyboard/mouse, and game controllers. You will put hundreds of hours into this game with everything there is to do.

Divinity: Original Sin 2 costs $12.99 and offers no in-app purchases or ads. You can play it in both single and multiplayer modes, and the save progress supports cross-platform syncing.

  • Download Divinity: Original Sin 2

Resident Evil Village

Following its initial debut on consoles and desktop, Resident Evil Village has finally made its way to M-powered iPads. In this horror survival game, you play as a father whose daughter has been kidnapped. She's being held in a snowy village with swarms of monsters guarding the place.

Just like its predecessors, you will be scavenging different locations to acquire resources you need. Though, this edition focuses more on combat, so you will be facing the aforementioned monsters more frequently. That's not to mention that your enemies may be more unpredictable. So they may attack you as a pack, wield weapons, and try to outsmart you. If you're overwhelmed or unable to defeat them, in many cases you could just, you know, run for your life.

With this release, you still get a dedicated space to store and reorganize your belongings. You also get to buy and upgrade some of them when you come across a merchant. If this sounds like your kind of game, you can download it from the App Store for free. Though, do note that to unlock the base game, you have to buy a $15.99 in-app purchase, and there are other optional DLCs that cost extra.

  • Download Resident Evil Village

Simulation

This War of Mine

This War of Mine is a dystopian game that will completely disarrange your moral compass. Which is more ethical, knocking out an old lady to steal what little she has left or leaving a starving child to die of hunger? If recent times haven't been dark enough, this is a game to check out. Can you survive the horrors of war by scavenging, building, inventing, and protecting yourself? The graphics (particularly the colors), soundtracks and effects, and storyline are almost guaranteed to shake up your mood's stability.

For $1.99 only, you can own this moving, artistic game. For an additional $1.99, you can buy The Little Ones DLC, which allows you to experience the game from a child's perspective — if you don't find the game depressing enough as it is. You can also pay $2.99 for Stories - Father's Promise, which features a father trying to save his daughter.

  • Download This War of Mine

Among Us

If you like the concept of Town of Salem but want something more advanced that doesn't solely revolve around texting other players, then Among Us should be a solid iPad game for you. Similar to Town of Salem, there are two teams, the good and the bad folks. The latter category includes imposters who have to quietly kill the good team members. There are also chatting and voting systems to discuss and execute players suspected to be imposters. As a good teammate, you have to work on several assigned tasks and avoid being cornered by a bloodthirsty imposter.

Among Us is available on the iPad for free, but it includes optional in-app purchases to remove ads and unlock more content, like costumes, hats, and more. Because this popular game is available across other platforms like PC, Xbox, and Nintendo Switch, you can play with your friends wherever they are.

  • Download Among Us

Fallout Shelter

Fallout Shelter has a special place in my heart. Despite it being a freemium title (which I tend to despise), it still offers addictive gameplay that doesn't pressure you into purchasing microtransactions. In this game, you have to build a life underground in a vault from the Fallout universe. The aim is to grow the population down there and keep the people alive, safe, and healthy. The gameplay is relatively simple, but things get complicated when you send people out into the wilderness for scavenging, assign people to certain rooms based on their expertise and talents, and have babies. It's a cycle of increasing needs that you have to keep in check.

The game can get repetitive, but I love its graphics, style, sound effects, and simulation gameplay. It's still an excellent, free game, and there's no harm in giving it a shot.

  • Download Fallout Shelter

Goat Simulator

Yes, that's a goat's tongue stuck to a flying helicopter. Goat Simulator is an absurd, open-world game for when life makes too much sense. For starters, you play a goat. What you do with that information is up to your imagination. You know how in Grand Theft Auto some people ignore the missions and just go around robbing/killing people for the fun of it? Now imagine that but as a goat. You can take advantage of the open world and just walk (or fly) around harassing people and causing chaos. It's a weird way to wind down and empty some built-up stress, but it's a very valid way nonetheless.

Goat Simulator costs $6.99 and offers plenty of in-app purchases for additional content/expansions. Alternatively, you could play the plus (+) edition on Apple Arcade. If you like it, you can also check the other Goat Simulator editions on the developer's App Store page.

  • Download Goat Simulator
  • Download Goat Simulator+

Puzzle

Monument Valley

If you need to wind down after a long day of playing depressing titles like This War of Mine, there's Monument Valley. Here, the rules of physics don't make sense in a way that makes sense — does this make sense to you? The mechanisms of manipulating the landscapes are unrealistic, but once you go through a few levels and get used to the game, these unrealistic mechanisms will become familiar and sensible. Your aim is to lead a little princess through geometric worlds by twisting them. You drag, swipe, and move elements around, which is for the iPad's large touch display.

Monument Valley is available for $3.99 in the App Store. It includes no ads and only one optional IAP, which unlocks eight additional chapters for $1.99. If you like the concept of the game, you can play its sequel — Monument Valley 2 — for $4.99 (no IAPs). It's worth noting, though, that if you're subscribed to Apple Arcade, you can play both titles for free through their plus (+) editions.

  • Download Monument Valley
  • Download Monument Valley+
  • Download Monument Valley 2
  • Download Monument Valley 2+

Samorost 1

If you're a fan of puzzle games, Samorost 1 is for you. This point-and-click game was released back in 2003, but it has been remastered and optimized to suit modern devices. As a PC game that won my heart as a child, Samorost obviously needed a spot on this list.

This game requires focus and patience. You just have to guide a gnome (of sorts) through different environments. To do so, you must solve brain teasers by tapping on-screen elements in a certain order. It requires few skills to learn, and the story is engaging and entertaining. Just use your logic, and you'll be done in no time.

Samorost 1 is available for free and includes no IAPs. If you like the game, Amanita Design has released two sequels — priced at $1.99 and $5.99, respectively, on the App Store. Both Samorost 2 and Samorost 3 have no in-app purchases, either. And if you're subscribed to Apple Arcade, then you can play Samorost 3+ for free.

  • Download Samorost 1
  • Download Samorost 2
  • Download Samorost 3
  • Download Samorost 3+

The Room

The Room is a puzzle game by Fireproof Studios. If you're a fan of investigating, looking for clues, and solving physics-based mysteries, this one's for you. The game features very realistic graphics with sophisticated-looking machinery that you must deduce to unravel the story. That's the concept across other games in the series, as well, so it's easy to hop between them.

You can download and play The Room for $0.99 with no ads or in-app purchases. If you enjoy this game, you can get the sequels as well. The Room 2 costs $1.99, although it's free on Apple Arcade. The Room 3 is priced at $3.99, while The Room: Old Sins costs $4.99. They all come with the same vibe and atmosphere of the original title, offering 3D graphics, an astounding soundtrack, and a well-thought storyline.

  • Download The Room
  • Download The Room Two
  • Download The Room Two+
  • Download The Room Three
  • Download The Room: Old Sins

Machinarium

If I could, I would list Machinarium twice here. Developed by the same people behind Samorost, this title is a genius work of art. Here, you're a robot trying to solve puzzles to get from one rusty, cyberpunk-inspired screen/location to another. The gameplay is very similar to that of Samorost, but it's in no way redundant.

Machinarium's controls are very intuitive and touch-friendly, so it's super easy to hop in regardless of if you've played Samorost. It is priced at $5.99, has no ads or in-app purchases, and exposes you to a surreal environment that will likely get you hooked. It truly is one of the best iPad games out there.

  • Download Machinarium

Battle royale

PUBG Mobile

In PUBG Mobile, you're dropped into a world with several other real-life players, and the aim is to be the last person standing. There's a wide variety of weapons and customizations, and competing against your friends only makes the game more fun. It's arguably one of the best battle royale titles available to mobile players, especially since Fortnite got delisted from the App Store.

PUBG Mobile is free to play, but it expectedly offers in-app purchases. Despite that, you can still enjoy the title just fine without buying any digital goods. That's especially true since the game is still in active development, so it's frequently updated with new content and in-game events.

  • Download PUBG Mobile

Call of Duty: Mobile

If you like the battle royale format but PUBG Mobile doesn't quite match your taste, Call of Duty Mobile might be what you're looking for. Brought to you by Activision, this game offers pretty much what you'd expect from a mobile battle royale and more. You can fight 99 other players in a large world to remain the last person standing. Apart from that, the game offers other modes with different rules and characteristics, including Team Deathmatch, Domination, and Kill-Confirmed. This turns it into a rich, central hub for fans of mobile multiplayer FPS games. You can also connect with and play against your friends if you all are willing to put your friendships at risk.

Call of Duty Mobile is available for free. However, it includes optional in-app purchases that can get pricey. If you'd rather not spend your real money on digital consumables, you can still enjoy the gameplay without investing any cent into the game.

  • Download Call of Duty: Mobile

Card

Hearthstone

Hearthstone is a collectible card game from Blizzard Entertainment. Like many other card games, such as Magic: The Gathering, it's about laying out cards and using them as "soldiers" against your opponent's team. Each card comes with a special power, a certain number of health points, and other statistics that distinguish it, like a character type. So you have to place and play your cards strategically depending on your opponent's deck to win.

It has simple yet engaging gameplay that is easy to master on the iPad's touchscreen. This title is free to download and play, but it includes optional in-app purchases as well. Notably, though, you can enjoy it fully without needing to pay.

  • Download Hearthstone

Threes!

After playing Super Hexagon, you will need a fix. Threes! is one great option for that. If you're unfamiliar with it, in this title you have to swipe numbered tiles vertically and/or horizontally. The aim is to increase the highest number available on a tile, as you merge tiles with similar digits to double their values. It doesn't take long to learn how to play this game, but mastering it can be challenging. You will slowly find yourself understanding the logic behind it to avoid running out of empty spaces and losing.

Threes! is available for $5.99 on the App Store and includes no advertisements or in-app purchases. If you'd rather not spend money on the game or like to try it for free before buying it, there are two other options to play it. The first is downloading Threes! Freeplay, which is a free, ad-supported version of the premium game. The other choice works if you're subscribed to Apple Arcade, as the company has released a plus (+) edition on that platform.

  • Download Threes!
  • Download Threes! Freeplay
  • Download Threes!+

Casual

Alto's Adventure

Alto's Adventure is easily one of the best iPad games thanks to its breathtaking graphics that are almost guaranteed to help you wind down after a stressful day — particularly when paired with its original soundtrack. It's both a snowboarding game and an artistic masterpiece that is worthy of the iPad's large display. Additionally, it has a one-button control system, making it exceptionally easy to play on the iPad's touchscreen.

If you enjoy this game, you can also check Alto's Odyssey, which was released later and features sand-boarding. The two games are not related when it comes to the story, so you can play either or both in any order. Each costs $4.99 on the App Store, and they include no ads or in-app purchases. If you're subscribed to Apple Arcade, you can play the Remastered editions of both titles for free.

  • Download Alto's Adventure
  • Download Alto's Adventure — Remastered
  • Download Alto's Odyssey
  • Download Alto's Odyssey — Remastered

Super Hexagon

Super Hexagon was released a decade ago, but it still rightfully earns a spot on this list of the best iPad games. This title works best with touch controls, and the big screen makes the experience more immersive. As a result, playing it on an iPad would make sense the most.

The aim of this game — just like many other games — is to survive. Do you see that little triangle over there, towards the middle? That lil fella is you, and you must stay alive. You can do this by bumping into the approaching barriers. In this game, you press either on the left or right side to shift the triangle's direction/motion. So you basically have to slip through the tiny cracks between the invasive barriers to stay in the clear. Does this sound easy or simple to you? I won't subjectively comment on the matter. Instead, I'm going to share the difficulty levels included in this game — hard, harder, hardest, hardester, hardestest, and hardestestest.

This gem costs a mere $2.99 on the App Store, has no ads or in-app purchases, and doesn't get boring — assuming it's your type. It's ideal for when you want simple gaming mechanisms/controls, but also have an urge for some grind and stress.

  • Download Super Hexagon

Strategy

Town of Salem - The Coven

Lying in real life — especially about murder — is frowned upon. In Town of Salem, however, you're encouraged to lie because otherwise, you'll lose (your head). This game is based on chatting with other players from all over the world. The servers automatically assign roles to each player, and each role is divided into two main categories: Townies and Mafia. Members belonging to the latter category can identify each other and secretly chat during the night. Townies, on the other hand, remain in the dark.

Every night, members of the Mafia could attempt to kill a Townie. The next morning — through the group chat that connects all players — Townies must defend themselves and try to identify each other. Once there's a Mafia suspect, everyone gets to vote on whether to hang them or not. After the player dies, the server reveals whether that was in fact a Mafia member or an innocent, misjudged Townie.

The game has several playing modes with plenty of additions, cosmetics, DLCs, and more. It's available for free, but it has ads and plenty of IAPs for additional content and privileges. It's worth noting, though, that even without paying for the in-app purchases, the game is fully enjoyable in its free form.

  • Download Town of Salem - The Coven

Really Bad Chess

Do you know those dreams that make no sense at all? In a way, that's what Really Bad Chess is. As its name suggests, it's a chess game that is... really bad. You and your opponent receive a random assortment of chess pieces, rather than the standard, classic arrangement. This adds more to the fun as recycled strategies simply don't work in this game. You will have to think hard and come up with a new approach every single match.

The game is available for free but restricts certain features to paid players. For a one-time, $3.99 in-app purchase, users can remove ads, unlock local multiplayer mode, customize the palette, and take advantage of other features. The game remains totally playable even without paying, thanks to AI-ranked challenges. If you're subscribed to Apple Arcade, you can alternatively enjoy the full game experience for free through its plus (+) edition.

  • Download Really Bad Chess
  • Download Really Bad Chess+

Sid Meier's Civilization VI

Civilization VI is also one of the notable PC titles that have made it to mobile. The aim of this turn-based game is to build a civilization that lasts through different time eras. You will start at the Stone Age and slowly progress and advance your technologies. It's a great pick for those who like strategy games.

Civilization VI is available as a free download. However, after playing 60 turns, you will need to purchase the full game for $9.99. Apart from this in-app purchase, there are several additional DLCs that you can buy to expand the content available in this game. If you already own this title on a different platform, you can seamlessly keep your progress in sync on all of your devices.

  • Download Sid Meier's Civilization VI

The iPad blends portability with the big screen, allowing you to easily play games on the go, without limiting yourself to the small smartphone screen. While they don't support Mac games in general, they do have a rich library of premium titles that belong to different genres, as our comprehensive list reflects.

Nowadays, we can easily enjoy high-end titles on a mobile operating system, such as iPadOS 17. And some developers continue to work on bringing their desktop titles to mobile platforms. The mobile gaming future truly looks promising.

Apple iPad Air (2022)
$500 $599 Save $99

The 2022 5th-generation iPad Air is powered by the Apple M1 processor, it supports 5G for the first time, and it comes in an array of beautiful colors.

$600 at Best Buy $500 at Amazon $599 at Apple

  • Download Don't Starve: Pocket Edition+
  • Download Don't Starve: Shipwrecked
